options.A: debit motor vehicle account; credit bank account
options.B: debit motor vehicle expense account; credit bank account
options.C: debit purchases account; credit Things Caricom Motors Co. account
options.D: debit motor vehicle account; credit Things Caricom Motors Co. account

questions.explanation: A motor van for business use is a non-current asset, so debit motor vehicle account. Since it was bought on credit, the supplier becomes a creditor, so credit Things Caricom Motors Co.

distractors.A: Bank was not used because the purchase was on credit.
distractors.B: A motor van is an asset, not an expense.
distractors.C: Purchases account is for goods bought for resale, not non-current assets.

questions.stem:
Item 25 refers to the following information extracted from the books of a firm.

Closing inventory$300
Accounts receivable$1 700
Cash at bank$2 000
Sales$5 050
Purchases$3 020
Accounts payable$1 000

The current ratio is
questions.explanation: Current assets = closing inventory + receivables + cash = $300 + $1 700 + $2 000 = $4 000. Current liabilities = accounts payable = $1 000. Sales and purchases are income-statement items, not current assets/liabilities, so they're ignored. Current ratio = $4 000 : $1 000 = 4 : 1.

distractors.A: 2:1 would be the ratio if you only counted one of the three current assets against the payables.
distractors.C: 6:1 — likely from adding sales or purchases into current assets by mistake.
distractors.D: 9:1 — adds in sales/purchases or treats the wrong figure as the liability.

questions.subtopic: Cost of goods available for sale
questions.stem: Using the balances (opening inventory 8000, with sales and gross profit information producing cost of sales 9000 and closing inventory 15000), what is the cost of goods available for sale?
questions.explanation: Cost of goods available for sale = Cost of goods sold + Closing inventory = 9000 + 15000 = 24000.

distractors.A: $7 000 omits inventory adjustments.
distractors.B: $9 000 is cost of goods sold, not goods available.
distractors.D: $30 000 is the sales figure.

questions.stem: Using sales of 20000 and gross profit of 11000 (with opening inventory 8000), what is the cost of goods sold?
questions.explanation: Cost of goods sold = Sales - Gross profit = 20000 - 11000 = 9000.

distractors.A: $7 000 understates COGS.
distractors.C: $24 000 is cost of goods available for sale.
distractors.D: $30 000 is unrelated to the calculation.

questions.subtopic: Recording mixed cash/credit purchase of asset
questions.stem: S. Thomas purchased a machine for $3 250 from Grell & Co. Ltd, paying $1 250 in cash and promising the balance in ten days. What is the correct procedure for recording this transaction?

options.B: Debit purchases 3250; Credit Grell & Co. Ltd 3250
options.C: Debit machinery 3250; Credit cash 1250; Credit Grell & Co. Ltd 2000
options.D: Debit Grell & Co. Ltd 2000; Debit cash 1250; Credit purchases 3250

questions.explanation: Machinery (asset) is debited with the full cost $3 250. Cash is credited for the amount paid ($1 250) and Grell & Co. Ltd (creditor) is credited for the balance owing ($2 000).

distractors.A: Cash should only be credited with 1250, not 2000.
distractors.B: Machinery is a non-current asset, not purchases.
distractors.D: Has the entries the wrong way round; Grell & Co. is the creditor.

questions.stem: On 1 January 2024, $10 000 was paid towards an advertising campaign. Of this amount, $7 000 was used by 31 December 2024. What was the amount of advertising prepaid at the end of the year?
questions.explanation: Prepaid amount = Amount paid - Amount used = 10000 - 7000 = 3000.

distractors.B: $7 000 is the amount used (the expense).
distractors.C: $10 000 is the total payment.
distractors.D: $17 000 is the sum of payment and expense.

questions.topic: Limited liability company / co-operative / NPO statements
questions.stem: Which of the following features is associated with private limited companies?

options.D: Companies shares are not sold publicly.

questions.explanation: A defining feature of a private limited company is that its shares cannot be offered to the general public for sale.

distractors.A: Family ownership is common but not a defining legal feature.
distractors.B: Private limited companies do not publish financial statements to the same extent as public companies.
distractors.C: Government agencies do not typically purchase shares in private limited companies as a defining feature.

questions.stem: M. Brown wishes to increase his allowance for doubtful debts from 8% to 10%. Accounts receivable at the end of the year totalled $10 000, whilst the balance in the allowance for doubtful debts account at the start of the year was $800. Which of the following would be the correct entries to record the provision for the year?

options.A: Debit profit and loss 200; Credit allowance for doubtful debts 200
options.B: Debit allowance for doubtful debts 200; Credit profit and loss 200
options.C: Debit profit and loss 800; Credit allowance for doubtful debts 800
options.D: Debit allowance for doubtful debts 200; Credit profit and loss 800

questions.explanation: Required allowance = 10% x 10000 = 1000. Existing allowance = 800, so an increase of 200 is needed. Debit profit and loss 200 (expense) and credit allowance for doubtful debts 200.

distractors.B: Reverses the entries; this would be for a decrease in allowance.
distractors.C: $800 is the opening balance, not the increase.
distractors.D: Mixes incorrect amounts on each side.

questions.stem: Goods were sold on credit to John Brown. Which of the following double entries is a correct record of this transaction?

options.A: Debit sales; Credit John Brown's account
options.B: Debit John Brown's account; Credit sales
options.C: Debit John Brown's account; Debit cash
options.D: Debit cash; Credit John Brown's account

questions.explanation: A credit sale increases the debtor (John Brown's account, debit) and increases sales (credit).

distractors.A: Reverses the correct entries.
distractors.C: No cash is involved in a credit sale.
distractors.D: No cash is received in a credit sale.

questions.subtopic: Journal entry for credit purchase of asset
questions.stem: Ms Hoyte, a grocer, bought a computer on credit from Tan Company. In Ms Hoyte's books, which of the following journal entries would be made for this transaction?
questions.explanation: The computer is a non-current asset, so debit computer account. Since it was bought on credit, Tan Company is a creditor, so credit Tan Company.

distractors.A: Computer for business use is not a 'purchases' (goods for resale) item.
distractors.C: Reverses the correct entries.
distractors.D: Cash was not paid because the purchase was on credit.

questions.stem: Using the information for KD Ltd (opening capital 3000, net profit 1500, drawings 500), what is the closing capital of KD Ltd?
questions.explanation: Closing capital = Opening capital + Net profit - Drawings = 3000 + 1500 - 500 = 4000.

distractors.A: $1 000 is unrelated to the calculation.
distractors.B: $2 000 fails to add the profit correctly.
distractors.D: $5 000 ignores drawings.

questions.stem: A businessman offers a reduced amount for prompt payment for goods purchased. Under which column in his three-column cash book would this reduction be entered?
questions.explanation: A discount received by the buyer for early payment is recorded in the discount received column on the credit side of the cash book.

distractors.A: Bank column records actual money movements.
distractors.B: Cash column records actual money movements.
distractors.C: Discount allowed applies when the business itself gives discount to its customers.

questions.stem: The total credit of the bank column of a three-column cash book is greater than the total debit. The MOST likely reason for this situation is that:

options.B: transactions were omitted from the debit side
options.C: some figures were overstated on the credit side
options.D: the correct transactions were entered on the credit side

questions.explanation: When payments (credits) from the bank exceed receipts (debits), the business has spent more than it has received - a bank overdraft.

distractors.B: Omitted debits would be an error, but overdraft is the normal accounting reason.
distractors.C: Overstated credits would be an error, not the most likely cause.
distractors.D: Correct entries on the credit side do not by themselves explain the imbalance.

questions.stem: Ash Bicycle Shop recorded the following insurance information for 2024: Jan 1 prepaid insurance 250, Mar 31 paid insurance by cash 1000, Aug 31 paid insurance by cheque 800, Dec 31 prepaid insurance 150. How much should be recorded as insurance expense at the end of the year?
questions.explanation: Insurance expense = Opening prepayment + Payments - Closing prepayment = 250 + 1000 + 800 - 150 = 1900.

distractors.A: $1 800 omits a prepayment adjustment.
distractors.C: $2 050 adds the closing prepayment instead of subtracting it.
distractors.D: $2 200 ignores opening prepayment.

questions.stem: Janice purchased 20 items priced at $10 each, receiving a 5% trade discount. One month later, she paid her bill by cheque, taking advantage of a 10% cash discount offer. Which of the following amounts would Janice have to pay if she had missed the discount period?
questions.explanation: List price = 20 x 10 = 200. After 5% trade discount, invoice price = 200 x 0.95 = 190. If the cash discount period is missed, Janice pays the full invoice price of $190.

distractors.A: $171 includes both trade and cash discounts.
distractors.B: $180 uses a 10% trade discount, not 5%.
distractors.D: $200 is the original list price.

questions.stem: Using PC Bank's statement for KD Ltd (Dec 1 bal b/f 60 credit; cheque 90 credit; Unit Trust standing order 100 debit; P. Palmer credit transfer 30 credit; bank charges 85 debit), what is the balance as per bank statement on December 29?
questions.explanation: Running balance: 60 + 90 - 100 + 30 - 85 = -5. From the bank's perspective, a negative customer balance is shown as a debit balance of $5 (i.e., overdrawn).

distractors.B: Credit $5 has the sign reversed.
distractors.C: Debit $65 results from miscalculation.
distractors.D: Credit $125 ignores the debits.

questions.stem: Credit sales which have not been paid up must be included in the total sales figure at the end of the period. Which accounting principle justifies this statement?
questions.explanation: The matching (accruals) principle requires that revenue be recognised when earned, regardless of when cash is received. Credit sales are therefore recorded in the period of sale.

distractors.A: Prudence relates to recognising losses early, not timing of revenue.
distractors.C: Materiality concerns whether items are significant enough to disclose.
distractors.D: Consistency means using the same methods over time.

questions.stem: A sole trader had purchases of 200 g @ $6 on 5 April and 600 g @ $3 on 10 April, with sales of 100 g on 12 April and 100 g on 17 April. Using the LIFO method, closing inventory would be valued at:
questions.explanation: Total purchased: 200 g and 600 g = 800 g. Sales total 200 g. Closing inventory = 600 g. Under LIFO, sales come from the most recent purchase (10 April at $3 per g): 200 g sold from the 10 April batch, leaving 400 g @ $3 = 1200, plus 200 g @ $6 = 1200. Total = $2 400.

distractors.A: $1 800 values all closing inventory at $3.
distractors.B: $2 250 is a miscalculation.
distractors.D: $4 000 ignores the sales.

questions.stem: F. Taylor's terms of payment for all his debtors is 4%, 15 days. The following debtors pay: I. Terry: 10 days II. Pearl: 18 days III. Vicky: 15 days. Which debtors will receive a discount?
questions.explanation: The discount applies if payment is made within 15 days. Terry (10 days) and Vicky (15 days) qualify; Pearl (18 days) is too late.

distractors.A: Pearl paid after the discount period.
distractors.C: Pearl paid after the discount period.
distractors.D: Pearl paid after the discount period.

questions.stem: How would purchase of equipment on credit be reflected in the firm's statement of financial position?
questions.explanation: Buying equipment on credit increases an asset (equipment) and increases a liability (creditor).

distractors.B: Liabilities increase, not decrease, when buying on credit.
distractors.C: Equipment is an increase in assets, not a decrease.
distractors.D: Both effects are increases, not decreases.

questions.stem: The gross profit of a firm is $7 200. Bad debts is $200. Insurance paid is $1 240, which includes a prepayment of $40. What is the net profit of the firm?
questions.explanation: Insurance expense = 1240 - 40 = 1200 (prepayment removed). Net profit = 7200 - 200 - 1200 = 5800.

distractors.A: $5 720 deducts the full $1 240 insurance and an extra $40.
distractors.B: $5 760 deducts the full insurance without adjusting for prepayment.
distractors.D: $7 000 omits the insurance expense.

questions.topic: Limited liability company / co-operative / NPO statements
questions.stem: In an effort to raise capital, a company offered 200 five per cent debentures at $100 each for subscription. Five per cent is an indication that the company will:

options.C: pay 5% of $20 000 in interest to the debenture holders
options.D: receive 5% of $20 000 in interest from the debenture holders

questions.explanation: Debentures are long-term loans. The 5% denotes the annual interest the company must pay to debenture holders: 5% of (200 x $100) = 5% of $20 000 = $1 000 in interest each year.

distractors.A: Debentures are debt, not share capital, so capital does not increase by 5%.
distractors.B: The company receives the full $20 000 in cash, not 5% of it.
distractors.D: The company pays interest to holders, not the other way around.
